---
title: "Seleccion Natural"
subtitle: "Intro Bio I (B0160) - Módulo de Evolución"
format:
  revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    incremental: false
    theme: simple
lang: es
---

## Metas

1. 
2. 
3. 

## Ser capaces de {.smaller}

::: {.incremental}
- 
:::

---

![](06-assets/sickle-cell-anemia-overview.webp)

---

![](06-assets/sickle-cell-S-gene-map)








# Parte 1. El problema

Start with a map.

![Image](https://images.openai.com/static-rsc-4/IJIslN2a4pz7TRiy_nic858IALx9Q3mLzH3ZY5e_0za-1hEpP8O_68Aui-ajlkzPRtYk4Y5SsFBJmKePdj308iEz3CvnvGAq0L4kcJhiqTnfkqQcCT3oRQSMaL-w0onYGKMmfdOqMNGeRopDxClED9MZfOhfLRRFIUBJZTDVDrBgBtpcDzIxF8ZYMC9rAVcz?purpose=fullsize)

![Image](https://images.openai.com/static-rsc-4/2nnzinnI_xnxFHxkml5im8Z0r_Xab_zuUrn685B2pG98iaAEqfvqGib13mMZxyYfBhjzOOrzBuemgGmMot33lCAmv09ej66SYcqoQloSWxu6H9fBN3cBIaOASHv9Fo0BJSEq9Vy1U4blmtbXDWiq2EnFE5cpemvBFUrBfEIX9SrpN65mTIYeQL7Sd7Eq044l?purpose=fullsize)

![Image](https://images.openai.com/static-rsc-4/Wn7z5lzx1v5lyO6b7t0m3HzNlxaaGWa1MiQPXtt5dVesW4VRce5vlm5Jw7LG9LHHA8h81_90lev6OMYnoGBp1koM5CFCz9qGS3HQdwQepUEZ58tHe8ktgymRCLNbhjivomeuiyYO5Yv_-nY66sTGvT_71jj-TB4wF3fTgy2rVc6RQCafvu47OPr2OEiNoJxs?purpose=fullsize)

![Image](https://images.openai.com/static-rsc-4/uNeU-ReqZZuXWontCb1StPyxh5SMvr_-Z8siUPdDcbWysmlpiU9_9_IlPpD_b6oq9049-4j0uPYJuEF5PWLP7ix0eNrRJXi8-y-pu6xF3DMUTwi-X5E_drgLG8S-7aFVDIMzSvjA5g__ZrCw0dBQQcv4GXxLHkqLVMEbEnX3N_ivnzpSZ896-FClOaDarqrj?purpose=fullsize)

Ask:

> ¿Por qué un alelo que causa una enfermedad grave sigue siendo relativamente común en algunas poblaciones humanas?

Students usually expect:

* "porque apareció recientemente"
* "porque no ha sido eliminado todavía"
* "porque la medicina moderna lo mantiene"

Then show that the pattern was already present long before modern medicine.

---

# Parte 2. La observación

Define the locus:

* A = hemoglobina normal
* S = alelo falciforme

Genotipos:

| Genotipo | Fenotipo          |
| -------- | ----------------- |
| AA       | normal            |
| AS       | portador          |
| SS       | anemia falciforme |

Then present the paradox:

* SS tiene baja supervivencia.
* Sin embargo S no desaparece.

Ask:

> Si SS es tan perjudicial, ¿qué debería pasar con S según nuestra intuición?

Most students will answer:

> debería desaparecer.

Perfect.

---

# Parte 3. La explicación

Introduce malaria.

Explain briefly:

* *Plasmodium falciparum*
* alta mortalidad histórica
* selección intensa

Then present the key finding:

> Los individuos AS presentan menor riesgo de malaria severa.

Now you have:

| Genotipo | Sickle cell | Malaria        |
| -------- | ----------- | -------------- |
| AA       | No          | Vulnerable     |
| AS       | No grave    | Más resistente |
| SS       | Grave       | Resistente     |

Students can immediately see the trade-off.

---

# Parte 4. Fitness relativa

Now introduce fitness.

Not absolute fitness.

Relative fitness.

For example:

| Genotipo | Fitness |
| -------- | ------- |
| AA       | 0.90    |
| AS       | 1.00    |
| SS       | 0.20    |

Stress:

> Los números son arbitrarios. Lo importante es el orden.

AS has the highest fitness.

Therefore:

* AA selected against (malaria)
* SS selected against (anemia)
* AS favored

This is the first time students see evolution as a quantitative process.

---


::: {.incremental}
- **Selección natural**: Cambio en frecuencias alélicas debido a diferencias en supervivencia o reproducción
- **Presiones selectivas**: Factores ambientales que afectan la aptitud de diferentes genotipos
- **Equilibrio balanceado**: Cuando dos alelos se mantienen a frecuencias intermedias por presiones selectivas opuestas
:::


# Parte 5. Simulación sencilla

Have students predict.

Start:

* A = 0.9
* S = 0.1

Question:

> ¿Desaparecerá S?

Most will say yes.

Then show:

No.

Selection increases S until an equilibrium is reached.

This connects beautifully to Hardy-Weinberg:

HW says what happens with no evolution.

This example shows what happens when one HW assumption is violated:

> supervivencia igual para todos los genotipos.

---

# Parte 6. Nombre del fenómeno

Only now introduce:

## Selección balanceadora

Definition:

> La selección mantiene múltiples alelos en la población.

Mechanism:

> Ventaja del heterocigoto.

This is a very intuitive place to introduce the term.

---

# Parte 7. Generalización

Now ask:

> ¿Qué pasaría si cambiamos cuál genotipo tiene la mayor aptitud?

This naturally leads to the other modes.

---

## Selección direccional

Example:

| Genotipo | Fitness |
| -------- | ------- |
| AA       | 0.5     |
| AS       | 0.8     |
| SS       | 1.0     |

Now S increases toward fixation.

Graph:

Frequency distribution shifts toward one extreme.

Examples:

* resistencia a antibióticos
* resistencia a insecticidas
* melanismo industrial

---

## Selección estabilizadora

Use a quantitative trait.

Human birth weight is the classic example.

Too low → mortality.

Too high → mortality.

Intermediate values favored.

This is conceptually similar to heterozygote advantage:

> los extremos son desfavorecidos.

Though one is a quantitative trait and the other a single locus.

---

## Selección disruptiva

Now flip the sickle-cell story.

Suppose:

| Genotipo | Fitness |
| -------- | ------- |
| AA       | 1.0     |
| AS       | 0.5     |
| SS       | 1.0     |

Now heterocigotes are disadvantaged.

Two extremes favored.

This is perhaps the cleanest way to introduce disruptive selection because students already understand the genotype table.

You can literally say:

> En la anemia falciforme el heterocigoto gana.
>
> ¿Qué pasaría si el heterocigoto perdiera?

And then show disruptive selection.

---

# Parte 8. Conexión con la definición de selección natural

Finish by returning to the core logic.

Variation heredable:

AA, AS, SS

↓

Different fitness

↓

Some genotypes leave more descendants

↓

Allele frequencies change

That is natural selection.

---

I would actually delay directional/stabilizing/disruptive until after students have fully understood the sickle-cell example. One of the biggest teaching mistakes is presenting all four selection modes first as abstract graphs. Students memorize the shapes but never understand *why* they occur. Sickle cell gives them the mechanism first, and then the graphs become a consequence rather than something to memorize.
